includedSources.name: Chamath / What I Read This Week
Robert Sinn / Charts of the Week

items.title: June SEP is a stagflation forecast in everything but the label
items.idea: The June 2026 Summary of Economic Projections raised Core PCE 60bps to 3.3% and headline PCE 90bps to 3.6%, cut real GDP to 2.2%, and pushed the return-to-2%-target out to 2028 (from 2027 in March). The Fed is simultaneously tightening its inflation forecast and weakening its growth forecast. The institution is signaling, in the only language it has without breaking communication discipline, that the soft landing is no longer the central case.
items.angle: This is the clearest macro framework call of the week. The gap between what the Fed's own SEP says and what consensus believes (71% of economists still expect hold) is the trade. Stagflation positioning — real assets, energy, gold, short-duration — over long-duration growth.
items.tweetA: The June Fed SEP raised inflation forecasts, cut growth forecasts, and pushed the return to 2% target to 2028. Consensus is pricing a soft landing. The Fed is not. One of them is wrong. It is usually not the central bank that blinks.
items.tweetB: Core PCE to 3.3%. Growth cut to 2.2%. Return to 2% target: 2028. Meanwhile Fidelity still has 55% soft-landing probability. The Fed's own numbers say stagflation. The question is when portfolios update to match.

items.title: 12 FOMC votes moved hawkish in 90 days — largest single-meeting shift in dot-plot history
items.idea: In March 2026, 12 of 18 FOMC participants expected at least one cut by year-end. Zero expected a hike. In June, that distribution inverted: 9 now expect at least one hike, 8 expect a hold, only 1 still expects a cut. Twelve participants moved from cut or hold to hike in a single quarter. Run the archive back to 2012 — there is no comparable single-meeting shift. The largest prior swing was December 2018 at three dots.
items.angle: The mechanical and institutional implications are both enormous. When 12 voters all move the same direction simultaneously, it suggests the Fed's internal models are flagging persistence risk that the press release language will not name. This is not noise. Hike probability is underpriced.
items.tweetA: In March: 12 FOMC voters expected cuts, zero expected hikes. In June: 9 expect hikes, 1 expects cuts. Twelve voters moved in one quarter. The archive goes back to 2012. Nothing like this has ever happened. The market is still pricing 'hold through year-end.'
items.tweetB: December 2018: largest prior FOMC dot shift was 3 votes. June 2026: 12 votes moved hawkish in one meeting. Either the Fed's models see something consensus doesn't, or 12 independent voters all made the same error simultaneously. Pick one.

items.title: Lumber-to-gold ratio at 0.149 — record low in modern data history
items.idea: Gold closed June 18 at $4,151.74 (+28% YTD, +70% from 2024 trough). Lumber at $633/1,000 board feet, flat year-on-year. The lumber-to-gold ratio sits at 0.149 — a record low. In every prior cycle where this ratio compressed below 0.20, the macro regime was characterized by weak growth, persistent inflation, and rising official deficits financed by monetary accommodation. We are in that configuration now.
items.angle: Long gold, short lumber-linked construction plays if this thesis holds. The ratio is at a level never seen before.
items.tweetA: Gold: +70% from 2024 trough, $4,151. Lumber: flat YoY at $633. Lumber-to-gold ratio: 0.149 — a record low going back to the start of available data. Every prior time this dropped below 0.20, the macro regime was stagflationary. We are there now.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching the dot plot. I'm watching lumber-to-gold at 0.149 — a level that has never existed before. When real assets (gold) crush industrial commodities (lumber), the market is telling you growth is fake and debasement is real. The Fed just confirmed it.

charts.desc: CHART WORTH SAVING — lumber vs gold long-term ratio at record low

items.title: Michigan 5-year inflation expectations at 3.4% — highest since 1993
items.idea: University of Michigan consumer survey June 2026 preliminary: 1-year inflation expectations at 4.6%, 5-to-10-year expectations at 3.4% — the highest reading since 1993. The entire credibility argument for the Fed's transitory framing rested on the assumption that long-run expectations would stay anchored at 2%. They are not.
items.angle: Long-run inflation expectations above 3% are a regime signal, not noise. If they stay here through Q3, the Fed has no choice but to hike regardless of what the growth data says.
items.tweetA: 5-year consumer inflation expectations: 3.4% — highest since 1993. The entire case for soft landing rests on anchored long-run expectations. They are no longer anchored. This is not a technicality. This is how the 1970s became the 1970s.
items.tweetB: Buried in the Michigan survey: 5-year inflation expectations hit 3.4%, a level not seen since 1993. The Fed has spent 3 years insisting consumers are anchored at 2%. They are not. Once long-run expectations drift, you don't 'hold through year-end' to fix them.

items.title: May payrolls 172K vs consensus 84K — labor refuses to crack as inflation reaccelerates
items.idea: May 2026 nonfarm payrolls: 172,000 vs consensus 84,000 — more than double expectations. Unemployment unchanged at 4.3%. This is the worst possible combination for inflation: labor is not loosening while prices reaccelerate. Core PCE printed 3.3% in April, accelerating from 2.8% in February. The Fed has no labor market slack to lean on.
items.angle: Double the payroll print while inflation is reaccelerating and the Fed is already hawkish. The rate path is not 'cuts later this year.' It's 'when do they hike.' Short the front end, own TIPS.
items.tweetA: May payrolls: +172,000. Consensus was 84,000. Unemployment: 4.3%, unchanged. Core PCE: 3.3% and accelerating. There is no labor market cooling to offset the inflation numbers. The Fed has no slack to lean on. This isn't a soft landing setup.
items.tweetB: The labor market is printing double the expected payrolls while inflation reaccelerates past 3%. In any other cycle this would end the 'hold' debate immediately. We're still arguing about it because the market priced 4 cuts in January. Those cuts are not coming.

items.title: Warsh FOMC debut: less forward guidance, dot plot stole the show — new regime declared
items.idea: Kevin Warsh's first FOMC press conference was characterized by brevity and reduced forward guidance. The dot plot did the heavy lifting: the Fed has moved to a hawkish posture not held since 2022. Dayan asks whether Warsh truly understands the root of the inflation problem. Week ahead data: S&P PMIs (Tue), building permits/new home sales (Wed), durable goods + PCE + income/spending (Thu), UMICH (Fri).
items.angle: The 'less forward guidance' stance is more hawkish than it sounds — the market can't be talked down by dovish language. Each data print now matters more. PCE on Thursday is the week's key number.
items.tweetA: Warsh's message from his first FOMC: I'm going to say less and let the data speak louder. The problem is the data is saying 3.3% Core PCE, 172K payrolls, and unanchored 5-year expectations. Less guidance + hotter data = more uncertainty, not less.
items.tweetB: New Fed regime under Warsh: reduced forward guidance. Translation: every data print is now a surprise. PCE, PMIs, UMICH all dropping this week. The Fed won't pre-anchor anything. This is a more volatile macro environment by design.

items.title: The Wage Slowdown Fallacy — wages aren't actually slowing
items.idea: Dayan published a note arguing that the narrative of wage deceleration is incorrect. This directly undermines the soft-landing consensus that wage-driven inflation is cooling. If wages are not slowing, the Fed's path to 2% requires demand destruction that hasn't started. Dayan has additional work on this following in the week ahead.
items.angle: If wage growth is not slowing, the 'immaculate disinflation' story has no mechanism. The entire soft-landing thesis depends on wages decelerating without unemployment rising. Watch income/spending data Thursday.
items.tweetA: The consensus view: wages are slowing, inflation will follow, soft landing intact. Dayan's counter: the wage slowdown is a fallacy. If he's right, the Fed doesn't have a gradual path back to 2%. It has a choice between engineering a recession or living with 3%+ inflation.
items.tweetB: I keep coming back to this: how do you get core PCE from 3.3% to 2% without wages decelerating? And what if wages aren't actually decelerating? That's the question Dayan is raising. Income/spending data Thursday may start answering it.

items.source: Robert Sinn / Charts of the Week
items.title: Gold peaked at $4,400 on FOMC day, closed week near $4,200 — Warsh hawkish vibes repriced
items.idea: Gold hit $4,400/oz Wednesday morning during the FOMC, then sold off sharply to close the week near $4,200 after Kevin Warsh delivered hawkish vibes. The $200 intraday swing on a single press conference shows how leveraged the gold trade has become to Fed expectations. US equities bounced back Thursday; precious metals continued lower. The divergence between equities recovering and gold staying weak is notable.
items.angle: Gold at $4,200 is still +28% YTD and the structural case hasn't changed. But the short-term trade is volatile. Miners underperforming gold adds another layer.
items.tweetA: Gold hit $4,400 on FOMC day, then gave back $200 in 48 hours on Warsh's hawkish vibes. At $4,200 it's still up 28% YTD. The structural case is intact. But this is now a volatile trade, not a safe haven — and the miners are diverging hard to the downside.
items.tweetB: Interesting: equities bounced Thursday after the hawkish FOMC. Gold didn't. When gold sells off harder than equities on the same risk-off catalyst, it means the gold trade is crowded and leveraged, not that the stagflation thesis is wrong.

charts.desc: CHART WORTH SAVING — gold weekly price action around FOMC + miners downtrend chart

items.source: Robert Sinn / Charts of the Week
items.title: Equity dispersion deepens: CAT/MU/INTC in bull markets while MSTR/PLTR probe 52-week lows
items.idea: The defining trend of 2026 in equities is dispersion beneath the surface. Caterpillar, Micron, and Intel are in bull-market advances. Meanwhile former momentum darlings MicroStrategy and Palantir are probing 52-week lows. The headline indices mask a rotation that is more selective than any broad macro narrative suggests.
items.angle: CAT makes sense (infrastructure, real assets, stagflation trade). MU/INTC make sense (AI hardware demand). MSTR/PLTR at lows while those surge is a value-of-AI-narrative vs actual-AI-hardware-demand split. Follow the hardware, not the narrative.
items.tweetA: CAT, MU, INTC at highs. MSTR, PLTR at 52-week lows. Same equity market, same week. The divergence tells you exactly what the market believes: real hardware and infrastructure win; pure narrative and software-adjacent plays get repriced. 2026 is a stock picker's year.
items.tweetB: Intel up, Palantir down. Caterpillar up, MicroStrategy down. The common thread: hardware and real-economy capex is working; narrative-driven AI plays are not. The market is no longer paying for the story. It wants the revenue.

charts.desc: Charts inside — dispersion chart worth opening

items.source: Robert Sinn / Charts of the Week
items.title: Gold and silver miners in grinding multi-month downtrend despite gold at $4,200
items.idea: Gold miners, one of the hottest trades entering 2026, have become entrenched in a grinding multi-month downtrend even as spot gold held near $4,200. This is a classic miners-lagging-metal divergence — either cost inflation eating margins, geopolitical risk discounts, or anticipation that spot gold itself rolls over.
items.angle: If miners continue to lag, spot gold is likely to follow. If gold holds here, miners eventually catch up or signal the whole sector is rolling over.
items.tweetA: Gold at $4,200, gold miners in a multi-month downtrend. These two things cannot coexist indefinitely. Either miners are early and spot gold is about to correct, or miners are wrong and the catch-up trade in GDX/GDXJ is setting up. This is THE chart to watch right now.
items.tweetB: Miners have been diverging from gold for months. In every prior cycle, sustained miners-lagging-metal has resolved in one of two ways: spot gold follows miners lower, or miners violently revert upward. We're at a crossroads. Warsh just pushed it toward the first scenario.

charts.desc: CHART WORTH SAVING — gold miners multi-month downtrend vs spot gold

items.title: Switzerland is not a peace deal — it's a bridge built while the river is still rising
items.idea: US-Iran negotiations at Bürgenstock under Qatari and Pakistani mediation. JD Vance + Steve Witkoff vs Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf + Abbas Araghchi. Uranium enrichment reduction from 60% to 0.7% is meaningful only if tied to a real verification framework. $6 billion frozen funds as a down payment. Lebanon is the chapter that could tear the agreement apart — Iran wants it included, turning the negotiation into a multi-asset risk book. The last major Iran nuclear framework took 597 days from interim to final agreement.
items.angle: Markets are pricing the opening press conference, not the 597-day path to implementation. The real trade is whether the $6B is actually released, ships move through Hormuz, and inspectors return.
items.tweetA: Switzerland is not a peace deal. The last major Iran nuclear framework took 597 days from interim to final. This one's agenda is broader, the regional backdrop is more combustible, and Lebanon hasn't been addressed. Markets are pricing the opening photos. That's not the trade.
items.tweetB: Iran enrichment at 60%. The US wants it at 0.7%. The gap is enormous. What matters is not the number — it's who verifies it, when inspectors return, what gets released first, and whether Lebanon stays quiet while they argue over centrifuge accounting. None of that is settled.

charts.desc: Charts inside — gold vs oil during conflict period

items.title: Hormuz as a probability market — oil doesn't need closure to price pain
items.idea: Iran says it has closed the Strait; the US insists passage hasn't been shut. Both statements are politically useful. Oil doesn't price only barrels that fail to move — it prices the probability that the next barrel becomes slower, more expensive, or harder to insure. A slower vessel schedule, tanker refusals, higher insurance, delayed cargoes can all produce the same economic effect as partial closure. Trump understands: he cannot afford a supply-side oil shock.
items.angle: Hormuz is not binary. It's an insurance premium story. Tanker rates and shipping schedules are the real-time gauge, not the diplomatic press releases.
items.tweetA: Hormuz doesn't need to close to price oil higher. A slower vessel schedule, one tanker refusing to transit, slightly higher insurance — same economic effect. Oil prices the probability, not the event. That's why watching tanker rates matters more than watching the press conference.
items.tweetB: Trump needs oil below $80 and Hormuz open. Tehran knows that. That's leverage. Iran doesn't need to shut the Strait — it only needs to make the market believe it might. The probability premium is already in the price. Every failed negotiation just adds another layer.

items.title: Iran peace deal is a fragile 'armed pause' — 35-40% chance of sharp escalation by year-end
items.idea: TMAD's read: the Iran agreement is a temporary pause. Core issues including remaining highly enriched uranium have not been addressed. Base case = fragile armed pause with ongoing proxy activity. 35-40% chance of sharper escalation by year-end. A genuine durable peace deal remains unlikely. The recent equity rally is described as 'mostly artificial' driven by Iran peace narrative + dollar weakness + inflation distortions.
items.angle: 35-40% tail risk on escalation is not small. Options on oil and VIX calls are natural hedges. The TMAD view aligns with Dark Side of the Boom: don't overprice the peace premium.
items.tweetA: The Iran deal: 35-40% chance of sharp escalation by year-end, per risk assessments. Remaining HEU not addressed. Lebanon not addressed. This is not peace — it's a pause with a timer. The equity rally it caused is 'mostly artificial' driven by dollar weakness and narrative.
items.tweetB: Iran peace deal: the core issues haven't been resolved. The rally it caused? Dollar weakness + suppressed volatility + Iran narrative working together. Peel those away and the market 'doesn't really want to go higher on its own.' That's a fragile tape.

items.title: Options market quietly building volatility insurance via UVIX calls at $3.50 strike
items.idea: While equities grind higher, the options market is quietly building insurance on the other side. Fresh call flow accumulating in UVIX (2x Long VIX Futures ETF) just above current price, with large positive gamma bar at $3.50 strike. Overall gamma exposure: +$9.8 million. This is traders using UVIX calls as leveraged protection against a volatility spike. TMAD's view: correction headed into midterm elections.
items.angle: Smart money is buying vol insurance through UVIX calls while equities are at highs. Most actionable signal in the email — someone is positioned for a vol spike.
items.tweetA: Equities grind higher. Meanwhile, UVIX calls at $3.50 are accumulating with $9.8M in positive gamma. Someone is quietly buying vol insurance through the options market. When smart money buys protection at highs, the question is not whether — it's when.
items.tweetB: The structure is fragile. Supportive flows are holding the market up short-term. The options market already knows this: UVIX $3.50 strike calls building. Heading into midterms, this setup historically doesn't resolve quietly. $UVIX $VIX

charts.desc: Gamma exposure chart — UVIX options market

items.title: Multi-manager fund PM turnover: median tenure 1.8 years at Point72/Balyasny, 3.0 at Citadel
items.idea: LinkedIn Insights: median PM tenure at Point72 and Balyasny is 1.8 years, Millennium 2.3 years, Citadel 3.0 years. Millennium reports 15-20% annual PM turnover as structural, hired ~160 PMs last year (3/week). Drawdown limits hard-coded: 5% from peak = capital cut by half; 7.5% = pod terminated, team gone same day. The industry is recycling the same pool of talent through the same platforms with enormous collateral career damage.
items.angle: Understanding pod mechanics is understanding why crowded MF trades can blow up violently — not discretionary decisions, but automated stop-loss triggers. When a pod hits 7.5%, positions get liquidated in the same market everyone else is in.
items.tweetA: Median PM tenure at multi-manager funds: Point72/Balyasny 1.8 years, Millennium 2.3 years, Citadel 3.0 years. Millennium hired 160 PMs last year, 3 per week. A rival exec asked: does the universe create that many qualified people annually? The answer is no.
items.tweetB: At Millennium: 5% drawdown = capital cut in half. 7.5% = you and your entire team are gone the same day. When a pod hits the stop, the risk system — not a human — initiates the wind-down. Understanding this is understanding why crowded multi-manager trades can gap when they break.

items.source: Chamath / What I Read This Week
items.title: GLM-5.2 from China's Z.ai beats GPT-5.5 on some benchmarks at one-sixth the price
items.idea: Z.ai (Beijing) released GLM-5.2 on June 16: 744B total parameters, ~40B active per token (MoE), 1M token context, sparse attention from DeepSeek. Beats GPT-5.5 on several long-horizon coding benchmarks at ~1/6 the cost. Ranked #3 on Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index — best open-weight model available. MIT license. Z.ai stock on Hong Kong exchange at ATHs since Jan 2026 IPO. Tang Jie (Z.ai founder) says China will reach Mythos-class capability before Musk's Q1 2027 estimate.
items.angle: The cost curve collapse on frontier AI is happening faster than Western valuations reflect. 1/6 the price for near-frontier quality means enterprise customers have a real alternative. This squeezes closed-model API margins.
items.tweetA: GLM-5.2 from China's Z.ai: beats GPT-5.5 on some coding benchmarks. Cost: 1/6 the price. 744B params total, 40B active via MoE. Open weights under MIT license. #3 on Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index. The quality-cost gap between open Chinese models and closed US models is closing fast.
items.tweetB: The best open-weight AI model in the world right now is from China. Z.ai's GLM-5.2 hits frontier benchmarks at 1/6 the cost of GPT-5.5. Microsoft is already evaluating self-hosted DeepSeek V4 for Copilot. The era of closed-model pricing power is ending.

items.source: Chamath / What I Read This Week
items.title: Microsoft weighing self-hosted DeepSeek V4 for Copilot — the Chinese open-model arbitrage
items.idea: Axios reported June 16 that Microsoft is evaluating a self-hosted, fine-tuned version of DeepSeek V4 as a lower-cost engine for Copilot Cowork. Microsoft is the largest OpenAI investor but simultaneously evaluating Chinese open-source models to reduce inference costs on its own products. Enterprise buyers are reaching for cheaper open models as token costs ramp and quality gaps close.
items.angle: Microsoft running DeepSeek inside Azure while being OpenAI's biggest shareholder is the clearest sign that open-source Chinese AI has won the cost competition.
items.tweetA: Microsoft — OpenAI's biggest investor — is evaluating self-hosted DeepSeek V4 for Copilot. That's the AI equivalent of Coca-Cola considering switching to Pepsi's formula to save on syrup costs. The open-model cost arbitrage is too large to ignore, even for the incumbent.
items.tweetB: If Microsoft is using Chinese open-source AI in its own products, the closed-model premium narrative is over. Token costs matter. Quality gaps are closing. The market is still pricing AI like it's a duopoly. It's not.

items.source: Chamath / What I Read This Week
items.title: Midjourney building a 60-second whole-body alternative to MRI — 500,000 ultrasound sensors, no radiation
items.idea: Midjourney announced a medical division and a full-body scan machine: 500,000 ultrasound sensors on Butterfly Network chips in a water-submerged ring. No radiation or magnets. Half-millimeter resolution with MRI-like quality. Generates 17 GB/second, needs 21 servers + ~2 petaflops. Currently 20 minutes; target: 60 seconds. Goal: 50,000 scanners doing 1 billion scans/month by 2031, installed in spas. First unit: San Francisco next year. FDA path starts narrow with body composition maps. Midjourney: $500M+ ARR, no outside investors.
items.angle: If it reaches 60 seconds and FDA diagnostic approval, it fundamentally disrupts radiology economics. The spa distribution model is clever — consumer health data at scale without hospital cost structure.
items.tweetA: Midjourney — the AI image company — is building a 60-second MRI alternative. 500,000 ultrasound sensors, no radiation, half-millimeter resolution. Goal: 50,000 units in spas doing 1B scans/month by 2031. The pivot from image generation to full-body imaging is not a detour. It's the same compute story.
items.tweetB: The most interesting medical device announcement of 2026: Midjourney's full-body ultrasound scanner. 17 GB/second of data, 2 petaflops to reconstruct. Currently takes 20 minutes; target is 60 seconds. Distribution channel: spas. If FDA approves diagnostic use, this is the end of $5,000 MRI scans.

items.source: Chamath / What I Read This Week
items.title: Anduril wins fastest US fighter production contract in 50 years — FQ-44 enters serial production
items.idea: Air Force Collaborative Combat Aircraft program awarded to Anduril + General Atomics. Anduril's FQ-44: prototype award April 2024 → first flight October 2024 → production contract June 2026 — fastest path for a US fighter in 50+ years. First new company to win a US fighter program since the 1970s. Arsenal-1 in Ohio: up to 150 semi-autonomous aircraft/year. Anduril: 9 years old, raised $5B at $61B valuation one month prior (double a year earlier).
items.angle: Defense tech generational shift crystallizing. $61B valuation is still pre-revenue at scale — the production contract is the unlock. Anduril beating incumbents to a fighter program is the clearest proof that defense procurement can accelerate.
items.tweetA: Anduril: 9 years old. Just won its first fighter production contract — the fastest path from prototype to production for any US fighter in 50 years. April 2024 prototype award to June 2026 serial production. First new company to win a US fighter program since the 1970s.
items.tweetB: Prototype to production in 26 months. That's what Anduril just did with the FQ-44. The US Air Force moved faster on this than on any fighter program since Nixon was president. When speed of iteration becomes a defense requirement, the incumbents can't keep up.

items.source: Chamath / What I Read This Week
items.title: Fox buys Roku for $22 billion — owns Tubi + Roku = the FAST market's dual leader
items.idea: Fox agreed to buy Roku for ~$22B ($160/share, 34% premium over June 11 close). Roku: 100M+ US households, most-used TV OS in US (ahead of Samsung). Fox already owns Tubi. Combined: Fox ranks 3rd in US TV by viewing share, behind Disney and YouTube only. Fox had sold its 5% Roku stake six years ago to fund Tubi; now buys the whole company. Key asset: Roku's automatic content recognition (ACR) data — sees everything played on every screen.
items.angle: This is a data + distribution play more than a content play. Roku's ACR data is the moat — identity-level viewing data across all apps on 100M screens. Combined with Tubi's ad inventory, Fox now has a closed-loop ad targeting system.
items.tweetA: Fox + Roku = Tubi + 100M households + the most-used TV OS in America. At $22B, Fox is buying the home screen and the viewing data behind it. Roku's ACR sees everything on every screen — Netflix, YouTube, cable, everything. That data is worth far more than the hardware.
items.tweetB: Fox sold its 5% Roku stake 6 years ago to fund Tubi. Now it bought the whole company for $22B. Combined they rank 3rd in US TV viewing behind Disney and YouTube. The FAST market just consolidated around one player. $ROKU $FOX

items.title: PreAct: compile computer agent runs into state machines, replay 8.5-13x faster with no model calls
items.idea: PreAct solves the key inefficiency in computer-using agents: they re-reason every task from scratch. PreAct compiles the first successful run into a state-machine program and replays it on later runs without invoking the LLM. Replay is 8.5-13x faster. Safety: at each step verifies screen matches expected state before acting; hands control back to agent if anything diverges. Only stores programs confirmed to solve the task from a clean state.
items.angle: This is the operationalization layer that turns agentic AI from demo to deployable. 8-13x speed gain + deterministic replay is what enterprise workflows need. Major implications for RPA replacement.
items.tweetA: PreAct: the first time an AI agent solves a task on your computer, it compiles the solution into a state machine. Every repeat run: no model calls, 8-13x faster, deterministically safe. This is what turns computer agents from demos into operational systems.
items.tweetB: The AI agent problem nobody talks about: they re-reason from scratch every single time. PreAct fixes it — compile once, replay deterministically at 8-13x speed. The test: it only stores programs it can verify work from a clean state. This is production-grade agent infra.

items.title: LLM as Environment Engineer: RL policy diagnoses its own weaknesses and designs the next training stage
items.idea: Standard RL pipelines require humans to manually redesign training environments between stages. This paper lets the current policy analyze its own failure trajectories and propose modifications to the next-stage training environment. Key finding: the RL checkpoint is a better environment engineer than the original base model — learning to act also improves the ability to diagnose what it cannot do. Closes a slow human-in-the-loop bottleneck in agentic RL.
items.angle: Self-improving curriculum design is a step toward autonomous AI capability growth without human-in-the-loop bottlenecks.
items.tweetA: New AI training paradigm: instead of humans redesigning the training environment between RL stages, let the model do it. The RL checkpoint is better at diagnosing its own weaknesses than the original base model. Self-improving curriculum — no human in the loop.
items.tweetB: The hardest part of RL for LLMs: between each training stage, a human has to redesign the environment. This paper replaces that human. The model analyzes its own failures and proposes the next training config. The trainee becomes the trainer.

items.title: Diffusion LLMs get RL reasoning via PAPO — benchmark gains from 4.5% to 42.2%
items.idea: Diffusion LLMs fail with standard RL recipes due to sparse rewards and trajectory drift. Process Aligned Policy Optimization (PAPO) adds step-level process rewards for intermediate guidance and entropy-guided re-enactment at high-uncertainty moments. Results: gains from 4.5% to 42.2% on GSM8K and MATH500 reasoning benchmarks. Closes the reasoning gap between diffusion and autoregressive LLMs.
items.angle: Diffusion LLMs are the dark horse architecture. If they can now reason via RL at comparable levels, the architectural competition opens up.
items.tweetA: Diffusion LLMs can now reason. PAPO gives them a stable RL training recipe with step-level rewards and trajectory alignment. Results: up to 42.2% gains on math reasoning benchmarks. Autoregressive models no longer have a monopoly on RL-trained reasoning.
items.tweetB: Everyone's focused on GPT/Claude/Gemini architecture. Meanwhile, diffusion LLMs just got a working RL training recipe. Benchmark gains up to 42x on reasoning tasks. If diffusion LLMs can match autoregressive reasoning, the architecture landscape changes entirely.

items.title: Stanford EDGAR Dataset: 152B tokens of SEC filings in clean MultiMarkdown for financial AI pretraining
items.idea: Stanford released SEFD-v1: 152B tokens of US SEC filings in layout-faithful MultiMarkdown from an estimated 550B-token archive spanning 18.5M filings. Less than 0.1% overlap with Common Crawl. Two derived benchmarks: EDGAR-Forecast (numerical forecasting) and EDGAR-OCR (financial table transcription). Fills the biggest gap in financial AI pretraining data — clean, long-context, non-web-scraped documents.
items.angle: 152B tokens of clean financial documents with less than 0.1% overlap with existing training data is significant. Whoever fine-tunes on this first has a real edge in financial AI applications.
items.tweetA: Stanford released 152B tokens of clean SEC filings for AI pretraining. 18.5M filings. Less than 0.1% overlap with Common Crawl. The financial AI models trained on this will outperform anything trained on web-scraped data for actual financial tasks. EDGAR-Forecast benchmark ships with it.
items.tweetB: The missing ingredient for financial AI: clean, long-context, non-web-scraped training data. Stanford just released it: 152B tokens of SEC filings in structured MultiMarkdown. This is the dataset that makes a genuinely financial-domain AI model possible.

items.title: SpatialClaw (NVIDIA): training-free spatial reasoning framework beats prior SOTA by 11.2 points
items.idea: SpatialClaw lets a VLM-backed agent reason through code instead of guessing from pixels. Agent writes Python cells into a persistent Jupyter kernel preloaded with SAM3 segmentation, Depth-Anything-3 reconstruction, and geometry utilities. Masks, depth maps, trajectories persist across steps as Python variables. Results: 59.9% average accuracy across 20 spatial benchmarks, +11.2 points over prior SOTA, consistent gains across 6 VLM backbones. Training-free and model-agnostic.
items.angle: Spatial reasoning is the bottleneck for real-world robotics and autonomous systems. A training-free framework that plugs into any VLM and gains 11+ points is immediately deployable.
items.tweetA: NVIDIA's SpatialClaw: instead of having VLMs guess spatial relationships from pixels, let them write Python code against perception tools (SAM3 segmentation, depth maps). Result: 59.9% accuracy on 20 spatial benchmarks, +11.2 points over prior SOTA. Training-free, works with any VLM.
items.tweetB: The key insight in SpatialClaw: use code as the action interface for spatial reasoning. Persistent Jupyter kernel with perception primitives as Python callables. The VLM stops guessing and starts measuring. +11 points on benchmarks, no training required. This ships into robots.
